coredata.dc:title: Foliar carbon dot amendment modulates carbohydrate metabolism, rhizospheric properties and drought tolerance in maize seedling

coredata.dc:description: Improving maize drought tolerance is of great importance for scaling up production due to food security and population growth. Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method with citric acid and ethylenediamine as carbon sources. Then, CDs (5 ml, 5 mg‧L−1) were sprayed on 25th day-old maize (Zea mays L., drought-stress, 35% soil moisture) for seven consecutive days (spraying ultra-pure water as control), after which the physiological parameters and rhizospheric properties of maize under drought were evaluated. Foliar sprayed CDs (5 mg‧L−1) could increase root exudates (e.g., succinic acid (14.5 folds), pyruvic acid (10.0 folds), and betaine (11.8 folds)), and modify microbial community. Particularly, the relative abundance of Pseudomonas, Sphingomonas, Nitrospira, and Conocybe were significantly increased by 344.4%, 233.3%, 126.2%, and 122.6%, respectively. The altered microbial abundance could improve soil available nitrogen and phosphorus by 33.5% and 16.8%, respectively, and increase plant water uptake by 37.2%. The change of exudate synthesis and microbial abundance could be driven by the significantly increased in net photosynthesis rate by 122.9%, and carbohydrate content by 35.4% in shoots and 113.6% in roots, respectively upon foliar application of CDs. Meanwhile, fresh weight of shoots and roots were increased by 62.1% and 50.6%, and dry weight of shoots and roots were increased by 29.2% and 37.5%, respectively. These results demonstrated that foliar application of CDs could improve the rhizosphere environment to enhance maize drought tolerance and even growth. Therefore, foliar application of CDs would be a promising strategy for sustainable nano-agriculture in response to drought stress.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CDs' characterization and maize growth promotion under drought. (a) TEM image of CDs (inset: size distribution); (b) photograph; (c) FW and (d) DW of Non-CK, CK, and foliar sprayed CDs at 5 mg‧L−1 under drought stress.

Root exudates stimulated by CDs and corresponding molecular pathways. (a) Principal component analysis in Non-CK, CK, and CDs. (b) A total of 103 root exudates whose levels significantly changed in the maize rhizosphere with CDs treatment under drought stress. (c) Heatmap of discriminated root exudates in the rhizosphere after foliar application of CDs under drought stress.

Promoted maize photosynthesis by foliar application of CDs under drought stress. (a) net photosynthesis rate (Pn), (b) ETR, (c) carbohydrate content, (d) Images showing ROS levels of maize with Non-CK, CK, and CDs treatment. (e) Mechanism of root exudates synthesis after application of CDs under drought. Confocal imaging of DCF fluorescence showing ROS accumulation in leaf with Non-CK, CK, CDs at a concentration of 5 mg‧L−1 under 405 nm UV light.

CDs modulated rhizosphere microorganisms. Relative abundance of (a) bacterial or (b) fungal phyla in rhizosphere of Non-CK, CK, and CDs. Heatmap of (c) bacterial or (d) genera in the rhizosphere after foliar application of CDs.

Nutrients uptake and mechanism for drought tolerance by foliar application of CDs. (a) The DOC; the contents of N, P in (b) shoots and (c) roots of maize in Non-CK, CK, and CDs treatment. (d) Expression of aquaporin genes in roots. (e) Mechanism diagram for enhancing maize drought tolerance by CDs application.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Improving maize drought tolerance is of great importance for scaling up production due to food security and population growth. Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method with citric acid and ethylenediamine as carbon sources. Then, CDs (5 ml, 5 mg‧L−1) were sprayed on 25th day-old maize (Zea mays L., drought-stress, 35% soil moisture) for seven consecutive days (spraying ultra-pure water as control), after which the physiological parameters and rhizospheric properties of maize under drought were evaluated. Foliar sprayed CDs (5 mg‧L−1) could increase root exudates (e.g., succinic acid (14.5 folds), pyruvic acid (10.0 folds), and betaine (11.8 folds)), and modify microbial community. Particularly, the relative abundance of Pseudomonas, Sphingomonas, Nitrospira, and Conocybe were significantly increased by 344.4%, 233.3%, 126.2%, and 122.6%, respectively. The altered microbial abundance could improve soil available nitrogen and phosphorus by 33.5% and 16.8%, respectively, and increase plant water uptake by 37.2%. The change of exudate synthesis and microbial abundance could be driven by the significantly increased in net photosynthesis rate by 122.9%, and carbohydrate content by 35.4% in shoots and 113.6% in roots, respectively upon foliar application of CDs. Meanwhile, fresh weight of shoots and roots were increased by 62.1% and 50.6%, and dry weight of shoots and roots were increased by 29.2% and 37.5%, respectively. These results demonstrated that foliar application of CDs could improve the rhizosphere environment to enhance maize drought tolerance and even growth. Therefore, foliar application of CDs would be a promising strategy for sustainable nano-agriculture in response to drought stress.

ce:section.ce:para: Drought stress is predominant among various abiotic stresses and could result in extensive agricultural production losses (~50%), and its impact on crop growth and production is highly deteriorating (Kurunc, 2021). Thus, improving the drought tolerance of crop is crucial for global food security.

Previous studies demonstrated that root exudates and rhizosphere microorganisms both played a vital role in enhancing the tolerance of plant against drought stress (Cesari et al., 2019; Song et al., 2012). Root exudates, such as organic acids (e.g., formic acid, lactic acid, and citric acid), and carbohydrates (glucoside), can help plant to resist abiotic stresses (Song et al., 2012; Xu et al., 2021). For example, formic acid and malonic acid can increase the content of available potassium by 87.3%–129.2% and root tips by 53.8%–76.9% in Brassica napus, thereby improving drought tolerance (Xu et al., 2021). Proline, ornithine, methionine, succinic acid, citric acid, and 2-hydroxyglutaric acid could increase phosphorus (P) uptake of rice by 11.5%–18.7%, promoting rice's drought tolerance (Bi et al., 2021). Several reports have proposed that organic acids exuded from roots had the capacity to recruit beneficial microorganisms (L. Tian et al., 2020). Rhizosphere microorganisms (e.g., Actinobacteria, Streptomyces, and Azospirillum) play a central role in plant abiotic stresses (Franciska et al., 2020; Madejon et al., 2021). For example, Actinomycetes and arbuscular fungi can secrete some antioxidants (aspartic acid and glutamic acid) to enhance the drought tolerance of plants (Franciska et al., 2020). Notably, the crop can release 11–40% of photosynthates into the rhizosphere, which could increase root exudate and improve the abundance of rhizosphere microorganisms (Walker et al., 2003; Wang et al., 2021b). This rhizospheric process (root exudates and rhizosphere microorganisms) could enhance crop drought tolerance (Bi et al., 2021; Franciska et al., 2020; Madejon et al., 2021). Therefore, these findings suggest that modulating rhizospheric process by improving crop photosynthesis would be an effective method to enhance crop against abiotic stresses.

Engineered nanomaterials (ENMs) have significant potential in promoting plant photosynthesis by improving the efficiency of solar energy, increasing the content of light harvesting pigment, accelerating the rate of electron transfer as well as improving the activity of photosynthetic enzyme (Hatami et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2019). For example, the chlorophyll pigments in Brassica napus L. were increased by 104.0% upon exposure to 20.0 mg·L−1 SiO2 ENMs (Kheyrkhah et al., 2018). The electron transport rates of Arabidopsis were improved by 27% and 31% after treating with 2.5 and 5 mg‧L−1 of single-walled carbon nanotubes, respectively (Giraldo et al., 2014). Among these, carbon dots (CDs), as a new rising star in carbon-based materials, have great potential applications in nano-enabled agriculture due to low toxicity, small size, and high quantum yield (Liu et al., 2020b). For example, CDs were considered to increase photosynthesis by absorbing a broad range of ultraviolet and raise the electron transport rate (Li et al., 2021b; Sai et al., 2018). CDs at 10, 30, or 50 mg‧L−1 can accelerate the growth of fenugreek by assisting the uptake of micronutrient (Waghmare et al., 2021). CDs (560 mg‧L−1) could increase the grain yield of dicotyledons (soybean, tomato, and eggplant) by 20% (Li et al., 2019). In addition, CDs can enhance crop stress resistance, for example, CDs can protect rice against salt stress or boost peanut drought tolerance through promoting nutrition assimilation (Li et al., 2021a; Su et al., 2018). However, it is still unknown whether CDs can maintain or even improve crop growth under drought stress.

It is known that CDs can improve the crop photosynthesis due to their outstanding light conversion capacity (Li et al., 2020a; Xu et al., 2020). CDs, as efficient light-harvesting agents, could enhance the electron transfer rate of photosynthesis in lettuce by 28.0% (Li et al., 2020a). Foliar application of CDs (3900 mg‧L−1) on lettuce significantly increased the net photosynthesis rate by 13.0% and soluble sugar content by 66.0% (Xu et al., 2020). Cernusak and Cheesman (2015) demonstrated that promoted photosynthesis could enhance drought tolerance. On this basis, whether CDs could improve crop drought tolerance via enhancing photosynthesis remains to be elucidated. Therefore, we hypothesize that CDs can regulate root exudates and rhizosphere microorganisms by enhancing photosynthesis and increasing the transport of carbohydrates, ultimately, improving the drought tolerance of plants. Maize was selected as a model plant in this study since 5% of the global maize planting area is facing drought stress (Zhu et al., 2021). Thus, this study is aimed to investigate: 1) the potential effect of CDs in regulating the production of root exudates; 2) the roles of CDs in modulating maize photosynthesis and molecular pathway of root exudates synthesis under drought stress; 3) whether CDs might influence the abundance of rhizosphere microorganisms; 4) the impact of CDs in maize growth under drought stress.

The CDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal method according to a previous study (Qian et al., 2018). Briefly, citric acid (1.1 g, Sigma Aldrich) and ethylenediamine (335 μL, Sigma Aldrich) in ultrapure water (10 mL) were heated at 200 °C for 12 h. Resultant mixture was freeze-dried to obtain solid CDs. The CDs were characterized by a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEM–2100JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). Photoluminescence analysis was conducted by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F–7000, Hitachi, China). X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were conducted by a X–ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Kalpha, US). Fourier transform infrared (FT–IR, Tensor 27, Bruker Optics, Germany) spectroscopy was also used to characterize the surface functional groups of CDs.

The soil parameters were same as our previous work (Wang et al., 2021a). Maize seeds (Zea mays L. No. 9 Shiyu) were obtained from Liaoning East Asia Seed Industry Co., Ltd. A total of 200 seeds were germinated in a growth chamber without illumination for three days. Each seedling was transferred to a pot with 1.5 kg soil and grown under nature light (25 °C, humidity 50%). The soil moisture (SM) was maintained at 75% for 20 days. Then, on the 25th day, the pots were divided into two groups: drought-stress (35% SM) and non-drought-stress (75% SM). About 30 days, maize was at seedling stage and often used to investigate the mechanism for providing theoretical support in a full life cycle study (about 125 days) (Wang et al., 2021c). Meanwhile, 1.5 kg soil and 35% SM were selected according to previous researches (Chen et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021c), which could meet the crop growth and drought acquirement, respectively.

CDs exposure was performed via spraying 5 mL per plant: CDs solutions (5 mg‧L−1) on leaves and control plants were sprayed with equivalent amount of ultrapure water for the next seven consecutive days (seven times in total). The total dose of CDs was 0.175 mg per plant and the soil was covered to prevent CDs running off from the leaves. Each treatment contained four replicates. The Non-CK and CK represented control group (without CDs) of non-drought-stress and drought-stress, respectively. Maize seedlings were harvested on the 33rd day. The probe soil moisture sensor (TZS–IW, China) was used to measure soil water content in all pots. The photosynthetic rate (Pn), fluorescence quenching, effective quantum yields of photosystem II (ΦII), Non-photochemical Quenching (NPQ), and electron transfer rate (ETR) of maize seedlings were measured by a photosynthesis analyzer (PP Systems, TARGAS–1, China) and FluorCam chlorophyll fluorescence imaging system (Beijing, China). Root length, tips number, and surface area were scanned by the root scanner (Regent LA2400, Japan). The carbohydrate content was determined by a previous method (Safařík and Šantrůčková, 1992). The details are shown in Supplementary text S1. The distribution of CDs in maize shoots was observed by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Nikon, Japan) under 405 nm excitation (Supplementary text S2). The nitrate (NO3 −), dissolved organic carbon (DOC), and available phosphorus (AP) of soil were analyzed by the published methods (L. Tian et al., 2020; M. Zhao et al., 2020; X. Zhao et al., 2020). The concentrations of phosphorous (P) and nitrogen (N) in plant tissues were determined by using an inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP–MS, ICAP–TQ, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Germany) and an elemental analyzer (UNICUBE, Elementar, Germany), respectively. The QA/QC details of ICP–MS are shown in Supplementary text S3. The ROS content was determined in vivo by CLSM, and the expression of SUT4 and aquaporin genes was assayed by quantitative real-time PCR (Supplementary text S4 and S5).

To collect root exudates, harvested maize roots were washed with ultrapure water (conductivity ≥18.25 MΩ.cm) and transferred to a flask containing 30 mL of ultrapure water. These flasks to gather root exudates of maize were kept in dark at room temperature for 48 h. The ultrapure water in the flasks was collected and concentrated with 20 folds, then the concentrate was filtered through a 22 μm filter membrane. 2-Chloro-L-phenylalanine (40 μL, 0.3 mg‧mL−1, dissolved in methanol), as the internal standard, was added into 360 μL of cold methanol and placed in Eppendorf tube. Then the solution was concentrated to solid with freeze dryer and the obtained solid was reconstituted with 80% methanol solution (1 mL, methanol/water). A quality control (QC) sample was prepared by mixing aliquots (15 μL of each methanol extract) of all samples. The blank was set as 80% methanol solution. Finally, samples were analyzed by high performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS, APS80-16PLUS, Thermo Scientific, Germany) analysis according to a previous method (L. Tian et al., 2020), and parameters were set in the Supplementary text S6. Method of metabolomics study is described in Supplementary text S7.

Soil bacterial and fungal community compositions were analyzed by high-throughput sequencing technique (Yin et al., 2018), which was carried out by Illumina HiSeq 2500 instrument at Personalbio Company (Shanghai, China). After sequencing the V3-V4 and ITS regions, the original readings (original PE) were obtained, and low-quality fragments were filtered to recover effective tags.

The maize roots with or without exposure to CDs (5 mg‧L−1) under drought stress were sampled. Maize transcriptome was determined at Personalbio Company (Shanghai, China). Briefly, total RNA was isolated via the Trizol Reagent (Invitrogen Life Technologies). Quality and integrity were determined with a NanoDrop spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific). After concentration, three micrograms of RNA were analyzed. Sequencing libraries were built with the TruSeq RNA Sample Preparation Kit (Illumina, San Diego, CA, USA). Briefly, the Oligo (dT) magnetic beads were used to enrich the mRNA with polyadenylic acid (polyA) structure in the total RNA. The RNA was interrupted to a fragment of about 300 bp in length by means of ion interruption. After the library construction procedure, PCR amplification was used to enrich the library fragments. Then, the library was selected in accordance with the fragment size. The library size was 450 bp, and the quality of the library was checked by Bioanalyzer (Agilent 2100). After RNA extraction, purification, and library building of the samples, the second-generation sequencing technology (Next-Generation Sequencing, NGS) was used to perform paired-end (PE) sequencing on these libraries via the Illumina sequencing platform. Expression level in each gene was measured by Fragments Per Kilobases Per Millionreads (FPKM) (Maher et al., 2009). Besides, HTSeq (0.9.1) statistics were performed to compare the Read Count values on each gene as the original expression of the gene. FPKM was applied to standardize the expression. DESeq (1.30.0) was devoted to analyze the genes of difference expression with screened conditions as follows: expression difference multiple |log2FoldChange| > 1, significant p < 0.05. Gene function annotation was conducted by comparison to the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG).

All treatments were presented as the mean ± standard error (at least n = 3). The analysis of variance (ANOVA) of the data was done by using Origin95_64. Statistical significance was based on probabilities of p < 0.05. For root exudates, all data analysis was based on MetaboAnalyst5.0 (https://www.metaboanalyst.ca/). The analysis of 16S rRNA and ITS sequencing data was conducted using QIIME 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization and promoting maize growth under drought of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The average size of CDs was 2.8 ± 0.5 nm with uniform spherical shape (Fig. 1a). The CDs showed excellent blue fluorescence at 365 nm excitation (Fig. S1a). The XPS spectra demonstrated that CDs were composed of carbon (C), N, and oxygen (O) (Fig. S1). The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum and FT–IR results both proved the existence of COOH (286.5 eV) and CO (288.3 eV) (Fig. S1). These hydrophilic functional groups endow the excellent aqueous dispersibility of CDs (He et al., 2018). Besides, a previous study showed that CDs can be easily transported into the plant leaves due to their small size (Qian et al., 2018). Herein, the CLSM images of foliar applied CDs in maize leaves indicted that CDs could penetrate mesophyll cell walls and plasma membranes, and distributed in the chloroplast (Fig. S2). Foliar application of CDs promoted the maize growth under drought stress (Fig. 1b). Compared with CK, foliar application of CDs increased the fresh weight (FW) of shoots and roots by 62.1% and 50.6%, and increased dry weight (DW) of shoots and roots by 29.2% and 37.5%, respectively (Fig. 1c and d). It was worth noting that root length, tips number and surface area upon exposure to CDs were increased by 167.9%, 157.0% and 95.6%, respectively (Fig. S3). These results suggest that foliar application of CDs can improve the survival and growth of maize under drought stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Root exudates stimulated by CDs under drought stress
ce:section.ce:para: The score plots from PC1 (90.7%) in principal component analysis (PCA) showed clear separation of Non-CK, CK, and CDs treatment (Fig. 2a), indicating that CDs altered metabolite profile significantly. The significantly changed metabolites were determined in univariate statistical analyses (Student's t-test, p < 0.05, Log2(FC) > 1 or Log2(FC) < −1). The significantly changed metabolites were classified into: organic acids and derivatives (9), amino acids (10), fatty acids and derivatives (11), sugars (8), benzenoids (14), phenylpropanoids and polyketides (11), purines (4), and others (36) (Fig. 2b and Table S1). Specifically, CDs significantly increased organic acid and derivatives in maize, such as succinic acid (14.5 folds), pyruvic acid (10.0 folds), and betaine (11.8 folds) (Fig. 2c and Table S1). Succinic acid and pyruvic acid are involved in tricarboxylic acid cycle (TCA) as well as release energy to promote plant growth under drought stress (Chen et al., 2021). Betaine contributes to protect cell membranes, improving osmolyte metabolism, and regulating ROS level (Ahanger et al., 2021). In addition, the secretion of L-glutamic acid, L-tyrosine, and proline was also significantly increased by 7.6 folds, 3.1 folds, 2.2 folds in maize rhizosphere (Fig. 2c and Table S1). L-Glutamic acid is an antioxidant enhancing crop drought tolerance (Franciska et al., 2020). L-Tyrosine degradation product can participate TCA to provide adenosine-triphosphate (ATP) for plant growth under abiotic stress (Ghoto et al., 2014). Proline could regulate osmotic pressure to maintain cell homeostasis under drought and salt stresses (Ahanger et al., 2021; Stefanov et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photosynthesis and synthesis of root exudates promoted by CDs under drought
ce:section.ce:para: Foliar application of CDs significantly increased Pn by 122.9% and ETR by 69.3% (Fig. 3a and b). Previous studies proved that CDs can increase the light utilization efficiency and are able to provide electrons for promoting plant photosynthesis (Swift et al., 2019). For example, lettuce leaves treated with CDs (7 μg‧mL−1) showed an increase of 25% in ETR (W. Li et al., 2018). Sai et al. (2018) reported that leaves (Nicotiana tabacum L.) injected with CDs can improve Pn by 18.0%. In addition, abiotic stresses can cause the accumulation of ROS, which could further destroy PSII and inhibit photosynthesis (Chen et al., 2020). Some studies have shown that CDs could eliminate ROS due to its physichemical structure (Wan et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2018). Our results showed that the fluorescent signal of ROS after foliar application of CDs was weaker (78%) than that of CK (Fig. 3d, Supplementary text S2), indicating that CDs might have reduced the over-accumulated ROS to protect the photosystem. Li et al. (2021a) also demonstrated that CDs can effectively alleviate the oxidative stress of Italian lettuce under salt stress via scavenging intracellular ROS. As well as there was no impaction on ΦII after CDs application (Fig. S4a). By contrast, the decrease of NPQ meant that more efficiency and energy collection in photosynthetic apparatus (Fig. S4b). Therefore, foliar application of CDs can protect maize photosynthesis under drought stress through light conversion and scavenging ROS. The carbohydrate content was increased in both shoots (35.4%) and roots (113.6%) due to the improved photosynthesis (Fig. 3c). Moreover, the relative transport gene (ZmSUT4) was up-regulated by 2.1 folds, suggesting an activation of carbohydrate transport to roots (Fig. S5).

It is known that carbohydrates provide substrates for synthesizing root exudates through glycolysis/gluconeogenesis (Jiang et al., 2021; Xiong et al., 2021). To explore the synthesis of root exudates interfered by CDs, transcriptomic profiles of roots were investigated. In general, 1507 genes (661 up-regulated and 846 down-regulated) were changed significantly after CDs treatment (Fig. S6a). The carbohydrate metabolism was included in differential expressed genes (DEGs, Fig. S7). The expression of genes related to phosphoenolpyruvate synthesis (Zm00001d010375, si946063f09, pco110419, GRMZM2G050077, pdc2, and me4) were significantly up-regulated by 2.1–3.5 folds (Fig. 3e and S6b), which could promote the synthesis of glycolysis, including monosaccharides (6-deoxy-d-glucose), disaccharides (maltose), and pyruvic acid. These products were beneficial for maize growth in adverse environment (Ahkamia et al., 2017). Moreover, pyruvic acid is an intermediate or substrate involved in other metabolic pathways (Guo et al., 2020). Besides, exposing CDs under drought stress significantly up-regulated the gene encoding isocitrate dehydrogenase (GRMZM2G033515) by 3.1 folds (Fig. 3e and S6b), which could increase the synthesis of succinic acid in TCA cycle (Camacho et al., 1995). The pyruvic acid and succinic acid are involved in the energy supply for TCA, promoting plant growth under drought stress (Vanani et al., 2020). Furthermore, pyruvic acid is a precursor for the synthesis of betaine, which plays an important role in responding to various environmental stresses (Zhalnina et al., 2018). The increase in pyruvic acid could be attributed to the overexpression of P4H4 and GRMZM2G366392 (2.6 and 3.0 folds, respectively) encoding prolyl-hydroxylase and S-adenosylmethionine decarboxylase (Fig. 3e and S6b). Up-regulation of genes GRMZM2G077054 and gln2 help to increase L-glutamic acid synthesis (2.3 folds) (Anton and Kutny, 1987). The expression of alanine aminotransferase gene (Zm00001d014258) was up-regulated by 2.0 folds (Fig. 3e and S6b), which may contribute to purines synthesis (Maurice et al., 1991). Previous research showed that some purines are capable of regulating endogenous hormone levels, increasing antioxidant enzyme activity and eventually improving crop growth under abiotic stress (Sheth et al., 2017). Overall, foliar application of CDs could promote the synthesis of root exudates via increasing synthesis and transport of carbohydrates to the roots, thereby enhancing maize drought tolerance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs modulated rhizosphere microorganisms under drought
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a total of 1,133,293 (bacteria) and 1,171,433 (fungi) quality sequences in the soil were investigated to analyze the rhizosphere microbiota composition. The read lengths varied from 239 to 442 bp (bacteria), 94 to 434 bp (fungi) with average of 378 bp (bacteria) and 269 bp (fungi) at 97% similarity level. Alpha diversity analysis demonstrated that there was no difference in bacterial and fungi diversities between CDs and CK, respectively (Fig. S8), suggesting CDs did not alter the rhizosphere microbial diversity. However, separate clusters indicated distinct differences of treatment groups (Non-CK, CK and CDs) (Fig. S9). Therefore, foliar application of CDs can improve photosynthesis and transport of carbohydrates from leaf to root, as a result, increasing pyruvic acid for synthesizing root exudates. Meanwhile, root exudates have a specific recruitment effect on microorganisms, and could alter microbial abundance under drought (Liu et al., 2020a; L. Tian et al., 2020).

As shown in Fig. 4a, Proteobacteria was the most dominant bacteria at the phylum level under drought stress, followed by Actinobacteria, Chloroflexi, and Acidobacteria. Compared with CK, the relative abundance of Actinobacteria, Chloroflexi, and Firmicutes of CDs application was increased by 87.8%, 21.4% and 400.0%, respectively. The predominant fungal phyla were Ascomycota, Mucoromycota, and Basidiomycota (Fig. 4b). Compared with CK, CDs significantly increased the relative abundance of Ascomycota by 48.4%, and significantly decreased the relative abundance of Mucoromycota and Basidiomycota by 74.3% and 79.6%, respectively (Fig. 4b). These results indicated that CDs did not affect the dominant bacterial and fungal classification, but changed their relative abundance under drought stress. Actinobacteria found in wet rhizosphere soil can enhance crop drought tolerance by promoting the decomposition or formation of humus and improving seedling vigor (Yin et al., 2018). Firmicutes is an important rhizosphere bacterium that can promote plant growth under drought stress via nitrogen fixation, phosphate solubilization, and plant hormone production (Naylor and Coleman-Derr, 2018). Ascomycota may promote plant stress response to drought and cold by utilizing more resource (sugars, polysaccharides) in soil (Egidi et al., 2019). These results suggested a promoting effect of CDs on beneficial microorganisms for maize growth under drought stress.

At genera level, the effect of CDs to improve maize drought tolerance on the bacterial and fungal in soil was further investigated. Compared with CK, application of CDs significantly enriched the content of Pseudomonas (344.4%), Sphingomonas (233.3%), Rhodoplanes (133.3%), Nitrospira (126.2%), and Gemmatimonas (71.7%) (Fig. 4c). Pseudomonas can help crop survive under certain stress conditions by increasing the degradation of various substrates and producing more vital compounds, such as antibiotics and osmotic substance (Park and Kim, 2021). Luo et al. (2020) found that Sphingomonas can enhance the drought tolerance and the growth of Arabidopsis thaliana through the strain-released volatile organic compounds. The foliar application of CDs had obvious positive effect on fungal genera, such as changing the predominant fungal composition in the maize rhizosphere. More specifically, the abundance of Conocybe, Ascobolus, and Fusarium were significantly increased by 122.6%, 53.0% and 38.1%, respectively (Fig. 4d). Conocybe is beneficial for cellulose degradation, polysaccharides production, and pathogenic fungi inhibition (Luo et al., 2009). A recent study proved that Fusarium can promote Arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi symbiotic effect and alleviate the deleterious effect of salinity on wheat growth (Elgharably and Nafady, 2021). Thus, CDs increased the relative abundance of bacteria and fungi at genera level, promoting the release of antioxidants, production of polysaccharides, and inhibition of pathogenic microorganism, contributing to maize growth under drought stress.

Korenblum et al. (2020) reported that variation of rhizosphere microbiota regulating root metabolism and exudation is a plant stress response to drought. Schilmiller et al. (2012) demonstrated that organic acids and sugars accumulated specifically in the tip cell of leaf glandular trichomes were closely related to the composition of microorganisms (e.g., Pseudomonas). Notably, compared with CK, the abundance of Pseudomonas increased dramatically by 344.4% (Fig. 4c), suggesting that Pseudomonas could promote the synthesis of organic acids and sugars in root exudates. In our study, organic acids, amino acids, and sugars were the dominant components of root exudates (Table S1). The synthesis of these exudates was largely regulated by related enzymes in their synthesis pathway (Maurice et al., 1991). The chemical substances released by microorganisms can enter roots to regulate the activity of enzymes, thereby affecting the production of root exudates (Etalo et al., 2018). Therefore, we proposed that Pseudomonas may increase the activity of key enzymes for the synthesis of organic acids and sugars.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nutrients uptake and mechanism for drought tolerance by foliar application of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, the soil DOC in the soil was increased by 16.0% compared with CK (Fig. 5a). The variation of root exudates and microorganisms can regulate soil DOC through decomposing soil organic carbon in rhizosphere and producing colloidal extracellular polysaccharides, which enhanced intrinsic drought tolerance of plants (Padhy et al., 2018; W. Tian et al., 2020). Moreover, the content of available state of N and P (NO3 − and AP) in the soil increased by 33.5% and 16.8%, respectively (Fig. S10a and b), which might be attributed to the role of rhizosphere microorganisms. For example, Firmicutes could take part in N cycle, and P solubilization to increase the contents of NO3 − and AP (Naylor and Coleman-Derr, 2018). Nitrospira could promote the nitrification process under drought stress (Elgharably and Nafady, 2021). The amount of N and P after CDs treatment were increased by 21.0% and 63.7% in shoots, and by 14.7% and 29.6% in roots, compared with CK, respectively (Fig. 5b and c). The increased N and P in maize may be due to the improved gene expression of aquaporin in roots, which can promote the utilization of water-soluble substances by crops (Li et al., 2020b; Orieux et al., 2018). In Fig. 5d, the expression of aquaporin genes (ZmPIP1;1, ZmPIP2;5, ZmTIP1;1, and ZmNIP1;1) in roots were up-regulated by 2.0 folds, 6.1 folds, 4.1 folds and 9.9 folds, respectively, which could lead to an increase in water uptake (37.2%) (Fig. S11). Previous study demonstrated that rhizosphere microorganisms can stimulate the production of the phytohormone (e.g. abscisic acid) to enhance crop drought tolerance through holistic re-orchestration of water use in root (Liu et al., 2020a; Vries et al., 2020). For example, Vurukonda et al. (2016) proved that rhizosphere microorganisms (e.g., Sphingomonas and Conocybe) can play a significant role in alleviation of drought stress by releasing abscisic acid. Yue et al. (2017) found the increase of abscisic acid can promote the up-regulation of aquaporin gene expression. Therefore, these results indicate that foliar application of CDs could improve the bioavailability of N and P by varying rhizosphere process of root exudates (e.g., succinic acid, pyruvic acid, and betaine) and microorganisms (e.g., Pseudomonas, Sphingomonas, Nitrospira, and Conocybe), thereby promoting the growth of maize under drought (Fig. 5e).

Taken together, CDs could improve drought tolerance of crops by regulating rhizosphere processes, which is potentially beneficial for increasing crop yields. CDs, as a metal-free ENMs, showed good biocompatibility and low toxicity at low concentration (Li et al., 2019). Meanwhile, CDs do not increase the additional cost of agricultural production, and the cost of CDs application is far less than the economic losses that can be saved (Supplementary text S8). Thus, the newly developed CDs-enabled agriculture has potential applications. However, the dynamics and kinetics of maize under real world scenarios (e.g., alternative drought and extreme drought in life cycle) are still unclear because most of the mechanism studies were performed using plant seedlings (Yin et al., 2018). Previous research has proved that interactions between root exudates and rhizosphere microorganisms could affect plant phenotypes over a long time (Vries et al., 2020). Therefore, the dynamic monitoring of rhizosphere processes under different drought conditions should be investigated. Moreover, the fate of CDs in plants and environment is still unclear. It has been reported that carbon-based nanomaterials can be decomposed into CO2 in a full life cycle (Huang et al., 2018). H. Li et al. (2018) and W. Li et al. (2018) also demonstrated that CDs can decompose into plant hormone analogues and CO2 in rice. However, more researches are required to assess the fate and risks of CDs in agroecosystems.

This work revealed that foliar-applied CDs could promote maize drought tolerance by increasing the levels of root exudates (e.g., succinic acid, pyruvic acid, and betaine) and microbial community abundance (e.g. Pseudomonas, Sphingomonas, Nitrospira, and Conocybe). Besides, The Pn and carbohydrate content were increased significantly, suggesting that CDs can further regulate root exudates and microorganisms by improving photosynthesis. Moreover, these root-mediated processes of rhizosphere microorganisms increased the bioavailabilities of (N and P) and water uptake by root, contributing to the enhancement of maize growth under drought stress. Therefore, foliar application of CDs could be a promising solution for improving plant stress response to drought while maintaining or even increasing agricultural production.

Le Yue: Investigation, Data analysis. Xuesong Cao: Investigation, Data analysis. Jing Li: Writing-review & editing, Formal analysis. Xiaoli Zhao: Writing-review & editing. Fengchang Wu: Writing-review & editing. Zhenyu Wang: Experimental design, Data analysis, Writing-review & editing. Baoshan Xing: Writing-review & editing.
